stem: What is the largest integer that is guaranteed to divide the product of any \( [[m]] \) consecutive positive integers?
solution: Among any \( [[m]] \) consecutive integers the product is always divisible by \( [[m]]! \), and the block \( 1\cdot 2\cdots [[m]] \) equals exactly \( [[m]]! \), so no larger integer is forced. The largest guaranteed divisor is \( [[m]]! = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( ([[m]]-1)! \); it always divides the product but is not the largest such integer.
This is \( ([[m]]+1)! \), which does not divide every product of \( [[m]] \) consecutive integers.
This squares the count instead of taking a factorial.
This doubles \( [[m]]! \); the product is not always divisible by \( 2\cdot[[m]]! \).
This adds \( [[m]] \) to the factorial, which is not a divisor argument.
This subtracts \( [[m]] \) from the factorial, which is not a divisor argument.

stem: What is the largest integer that is guaranteed to divide the product of any \( [[m]] \) consecutive positive EVEN integers?
solution: Write the \( [[m]] \) consecutive even integers as \( 2k, 2(k+1), \dots, 2(k+[[m]]-1) \). Factoring out a 2 from each term leaves \( 2^{[[m]]} \cdot k(k+1)\cdots(k+[[m]]-1) \), and the block of \( [[m]] \) consecutive integers \( k, k+1, \dots, k+[[m]]-1 \) is always divisible by \( [[m]]! \). So the product is always divisible by \( 2^{[[m]]} \cdot [[m]]! \), and taking \( k=1 \) (i.e. \( 2,4,\dots,2\cdot[[m]] \)) shows this bound is tight: \( 2^{[[m]]} \cdot [[m]]! = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets the factor of \( 2^{[[m]]} \) pulled out of each of the \( [[m]] \) even terms.
This forgets the factor of \( [[m]]! \) coming from the consecutive-integer block.
This uses only one factor of 2 instead of \( 2^{[[m]]} \).
This uses \( ([[m]]-1)! \) instead of \( [[m]]! \) for the consecutive-integer block.
This uses one extra factor of 2 beyond \( 2^{[[m]]} \).
This uses \( ([[m]]+1)! \) instead of \( [[m]]! \) for the consecutive-integer block.

stem: For every integer \( n \), what is the largest integer guaranteed to divide \( [[a]](n^{3} - n) \)?
solution: Factor \( n^{3}-n = (n-1)n(n+1) \), the product of 3 consecutive integers, which is always divisible by \( 3! = 6 \) (and this bound is tight, e.g. \( n=2 \) gives \( 1\cdot2\cdot3=6 \)). So \( [[a]](n^{3}-n) \) is always divisible by \( 6\cdot[[a]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the factor of 6 entirely, leaving just the coefficient.
This uses only 2 of the 3 consecutive-integer factors.
This uses only 2 of the 3 consecutive-integer factors (the other pairing).
This doubles the correct bound \( 6\cdot[[a]] \).
This overshoots the correct bound by \( [[a]] \).
This undershoots the correct bound by \( [[a]] \).

stem: What is the largest integer guaranteed to divide the SUM of any \( [[m]] \) consecutive integers?
solution: Since \( [[m]] \) is odd, any \( [[m]] \) consecutive integers have a middle term \( c \), and their sum equals \( [[m]]\cdot c \), an exact multiple of \( [[m]] \); taking the block starting at 1 shows no larger integer is forced. The largest guaranteed divisor is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is one more than the count of terms.
This is one less than the count of terms.
This doubles the count of terms, which is not forced.
This is half of \( [[m]]-1 \), not the full count.
This is half of \( [[m]]+1 \), not the full count.
This squares the count instead of using it directly.

stem: The quantity \( [[a]](n^{5} - n) \) is always an integer multiple of some fixed number, no matter which integer \( n \) you plug in. What is the largest such fixed number?
solution: Factor \( n^{5}-n = n(n-1)(n+1)(n^{2}+1) \). The three consecutive integers \( n-1,n,n+1 \) force divisibility by \( 3!=6 \), and Fermat's Little Theorem gives \( n^{5}\equiv n \pmod 5 \), forcing an extra factor of 5; since \( \gcd(6,5)=1 \) this gives divisibility by \( 30 \), and \( n=2 \) (\( 2^{5}-2=30 \)) shows the bound is tight. So \( [[a]](n^{5}-n) \) is always divisible by \( 30\cdot[[a]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This keeps only the factor of 5 from Fermat's Little Theorem, dropping the consecutive-integer factor of 6.
This keeps only the factor of 6 from the consecutive integers, dropping the extra factor of 5 from Fermat's Little Theorem.
This doubles the Fermat factor of 5 instead of combining it with the factor of 6.
This is \( 3\times \) the Fermat factor of 5, missing the correct combination with 6.
This overshoots the correct bound by \( [[a]] \).
This undershoots the correct bound by \( [[a]] \).

stem: For every ODD integer \( n \), what is the largest integer guaranteed to divide \( [[a]](n^{2} - 1) \)?
solution: Write \( n^{2}-1=(n-1)(n+1) \); since \( n \) is odd, \( n-1 \) and \( n+1 \) are two consecutive even integers, so one of them is divisible by 4 and the other by 2, forcing \( 8 \mid (n-1)(n+1) \). Taking \( n=3 \) gives exactly \( n^{2}-1=8 \), so the bound is tight. Hence \( [[a]](n^{2}-1) \) is always divisible by \( 8\cdot[[a]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This only uses one factor of 2 from the two consecutive even numbers.
This only uses the factor of 4 from one of the two consecutive even numbers, dropping the other factor of 2.
This doubles the correct bound, overcounting the powers of 2 present.
This drops the case-analysis on odd \( n \) entirely, using only the coefficient.
This overshoots the correct bound by \( [[a]] \).
This undershoots the correct bound by \( [[a]] \).
